<li><strong>Yes</strong>, your team will work with you to continue your current medications and collaborate with your prescribing doctors if changes need to be made. A number of outside factors could impact your access to GAHT while in treatment, so <strong>please let your team know about prescription refills/changes as soon as possible</strong>. Examples of outside factors that can delay your medications include controlled substances regulations, insurance verification, pharmacy inventory and/or medication change requests.</li>
<li>If you already have surgery scheduled, your team will work with you to make sure you are mentally and physically stable enough to have a successful surgery and recovery process. Generally speaking, patients receiving electrolysis are often able to continue this process while at PHP & IOP programs, and generally need to pause these procedures while in residential treatment.</li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645812997516-63e3fd8e-eaf9" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645812997516-63e3fd8e-eaf9" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">Can I start hormones and/or get surgery while in treatment at Center for Discovery?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<p>Center for Discovery currently does not have providers who will initiate gender affirmative hormone therapy (GAHT), nor does CFD have surgeons on staff. We would be happy to assist you in getting referrals to outside providers who specialize in these areas of gender affirmative care. </p>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813022118-18222a66-4b5f" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813022118-18222a66-4b5f" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">I have a gender confirmation surgery scheduled soon after treatment - does my treatment team need to know about that?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li>Yes, they do! Please let them know when it is scheduled and sign a Release of Information for your surgeon. The whole team — including your Gender Affirmative Care coordinator and you — will discuss what will need to happen to make sure you are ready for surgery.</li>
<li>Your team’s top priorities are to help you live the life you want, including safely undergo surgery and heal appropriately to achieve the best possible surgical outcomes. Eating disorder behaviors can get in the way — particularly by depriving your body of the nutrients needed to heal — so it’s your treatment team’s job to help you recover so that you can achieve your goals.</li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1651006493103-9f5936d8-bfdd" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1651006493103-9f5936d8-bfdd" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What resources might be helpful for me?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<div>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li><span lang="en-US">Support groups:</span>
<ul>
<li><a href="http://fedupcollective.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="0"><span lang="en-US">Fighting Eating Disorders in Underrepresented Populations: A Trans+ & Intersex Collective</span></a><span lang="en-US"> – Online Facebook support group & monthly virtual support group</span></li>
<li><a href="https://genderspectrum.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="1"><span lang="en-US">Gender Spectrum</span></a><span lang="en-US"> – Online support groups for children & teens</span></li>
<li><a href="https://translifeline.org/hotline/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="2"><span lang="en-US">Trans Lifeline Support Group Directory</span></a></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Many LGBT centers offer support groups, events, and social gatherings in person and virtually. You can search for an LGBT center in your area through </span><a href="https://www.lgbtcenters.org/LGBTCenters" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="4"><span lang="en-US">CenterLink</span></a><span lang="en-US">.</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Eating Disorder Foundation – </span><a href="https://www.eatingdisorderfoundation.org/get-help/support-groups/?fbclid=IwAR3W8FvJeIc0ocTJMaNiMhfsoFcgCITozvFw53khER6S8a8K5uXq0iePtdI" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="5"><span lang="en-US">LGBTQ+ Support Group</span></a><span lang="en-US"> every Wednesday 4:30-5:30pm PST. This support group is ideal for lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer and gender non-conforming individuals at least 18 years old to discuss struggles with disordered eating, body image and identity.</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Eating Disorder Foundation – </span><a href="https://www.eatingdisorderfoundation.org/get-help/support-groups/?fbclid=IwAR3W8FvJeIc0ocTJMaNiMhfsoFcgCITozvFw53khER6S8a8K5uXq0iePtdI" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="6"><span lang="en-US">Trans+ and Nonbinary Support Group</span></a><span lang="en-US"> every Thursday 3:00-4:00pm PST. This support group is ideal for individuals at least 18 years of age who identify as trans+, gender nonconforming, intersex, and gender diverse, struggling with eating disorders, disordered eating, body dysmorphia and body image. (The Wednesday LGBTQ+ group is for those who identify anywhere within the LGBTQ+ community. The Trans+ & Nonbinary support group is specifically for those who identify as trans+, gender nonconforming, intersex, and gender diverse.])</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li><span lang="en-US">Help lines:</span>
<ul>
<li><a href="http://www.thetrevorproject.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="7"><span lang="en-US">The Trevor Project</span></a> has a <span lang="en-US">24/7/365 Lifeline at 1-866-4-U-TREVOR (866-488-7386) or </span><a href="https://chat.trvr.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="8"><span lang="en-US">TrevorChat</span></a><span lang="en-US">, their online instant messaging option, or </span><a href="https://www.thetrevorproject.org/get-help-now" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="9"><span lang="en-US">TrevorText</span></a><span lang="en-US">, a text-based support option. If you are looking for peer support, you can visit </span><a href="https://www.trevorspace.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="10"><span lang="en-US">TrevorSpace</span></a><span lang="en-US"> from anywhere in the world.</span></li>
<li><a href="https://translifeline.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="11"><span lang="en-US">TransLifeline</span></a><span lang="en-US"> (877) 565-8860 </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;"></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Resource websites:</span>
<ul>
<li><span lang="en-US">Harvard Law School’s LGBTQ+ Advocacy Clinic’s </span><a href="https://hlslgbtq.org/trans-youth-handbook" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="12"><span lang="en-US">Trans Youth Handbook</span></a><span lang="en-US">  – Legal resource guide that covers the rights of trans youth across such a wide spectrum of situations, including identity documents, school, health care, non-affirming care environments and work.</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">National Center for Transgender Equality’s </span><a href="https://transequality.org/know-your-rights" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer" data-auth="NotApplicable" data-linkindex="13"><span lang="en-US">Know Your Rights</span></a><span lang="en-US"> website</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li><span lang="en-US">Nonfiction books:</span>
<ul>
<li><span lang="en-US">Gender Outlaws: The Next Generation by Kate Bornstein and S. Bear Bergman</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">The Queer and Transgender Resilience Workbook by Anneliese Singh</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Trans Bodies, Trans Selves: A Resource by and for Transgender Communities edited by Laura Erickson-Schroth</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">The Body Is Not an Apology, Second Edition: The Power of Radical Self-Love by </span><span lang="en-US">Sonya Renee Taylor</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">You and Your Gender Identity by Dara Hoffman-Fox</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Mindful Masculinity Workbook: A Practical Guide to Healthy Masculinity edited by Rocco Kayiatos</span></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Gender Failure by Rae Spoon and Ivan E. Coyote</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;"></li>
<li><span lang="en-US">Fiction Books with Trans Representation:</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</div>
<div>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">Birthday by Meredith Russo (YA romance, trigger warnings for violence, transphobia, suicidality, substance use, abuse)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">If I Was Your Girl by Meredith Russo (YA romance, trigger warnings for violence, transphobia/hate crimes, suicidality, substance use, sexual violence)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">Magical Princess Harriet by Leiah Moser (middle grades, trigger warnings for transphobia)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">Felix Ever After by Kacen Callender (YA romance, trigger warnings for transphobia, bullying, substance use)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">Dreadnought by April Daniels (YA fantasy superhero, trigger warnings for transphobia, emotional abuse, violence)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">Cemetery Boys by Aidan Thomas (YA fantasy/romance, trigger warnings for transphobia, blood, death)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">The Prince and the Dressmaker by Jen Wang (YA romance graphic novel, trigger warnings for transphobia and outing)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">Upright Women Wanted by Sarah Gailey (YA dystopian action/adventure, trigger warnings for homophobia/transphobia, gun violence, death, misogyny)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">PET by Akwaeke Emezi (middle grades/YA fantasy, trigger warning: sexual violence, child abuse)</span></p>
</li>
<li>
<p lang="en-US"><span lang="en-US">The Mermaid, the Witch and the Sea by Maggie Tokuda-Hall (YA fantasy/romance, trigger warnings for torture, violence, blood, sexual violence, substance use)</span></p>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</div>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="vc_empty_space"   style="height: 60px"><span class="vc_empty_space_inner"></span></div>
	<div class="wpb_raw_code wpb_content_element wpb_raw_html" >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<a class="anchor" id="form-jump-faq-loved-ones"></a>
		</div>
	</div>

	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<h2>Frequently Asked Questions from Parents, Caregivers and Loved Ones</h2>
<h3>Answered by Our Gender Affirmative Care Coordinators</h3>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Dear Parents, Caregivers and Loved Ones:</span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">Thank you for being here. Your support for your loved one is essential in their recovery and well-being, and we appreciate the work you are putting into learning and understanding.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">We want to acknowledge that you know your loved one. And, your loved one knows themselves best, which is why our philosophy is to listen and follow their lead while exploring identity. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The question of “what is gender affirmative care” is answered in detail below, but we want to emphasize that the majority of this work is about </span><i><span data-contrast="auto">exploration </span></i><span data-contrast="auto">and </span><i><span data-contrast="auto">support</span></i><span data-contrast="auto">. We view our role as being present with your loved one as they explore new territory. We want to create space for them to safely feel emotions that are scary to be felt alone and to safely try out possibilities with the intention of establishing a long-term, sustainable trust in themselves and their recovery.  </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">The information below is intended to enhance your understanding of this complex subject, rather than to speak to the specific clinical recommendations for your individual family. We’ve answered the questions below using evidence-based research, personal experience, and professional experience treating a large cross-section of transgender and nonbinary people with eating disorders. The care of your loved one at Center for Discovery will always be individualized. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></p>
<p><span data-contrast="auto">We look forward to working with you.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></p>

		</div>
	</div>
<div class="vc_tta-container" data-vc-action="collapseAll"><div class="vc_general vc_tta vc_tta-accordion vc_tta-color-grey vc_tta-style-classic vc_tta-shape-rounded vc_tta-o-shape-group vc_tta-controls-align-left vc_tta-o-all-clickable"><div class="vc_tta-panels-container"><div class="vc_tta-panels"><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138043-e9ed8ad2-4c4e" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138043-e9ed8ad2-4c4e" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What does transgender mean?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul style="font-weight: 400;">
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Transgender, sometimes shortened to trans, is an umbrella term for anyone who does </span><strong>not consistently identify with their sex/gender assigned at birth</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">The term transgender is an </span><strong>umbrella term</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> that can encompass a wide variety of identities and experiences, including male, female, non-binary, transmasculine, transfeminine, genderqueer, genderfluid, agender, etc. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Some two-spirit people identify as transgender and/or non-binary; some do not.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="4" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">It is important to note that people</span> <strong>self-define</strong> <span data-contrast="auto">their gender identity (rather than being labeled by others) and have their own definition/meaning of these terms; many of the terms mentioned above are not interchangeable. </span><strong>We recommend that you ask your loved one about what their identity language means to them</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138067-3c4e4d77-431f" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138067-3c4e4d77-431f" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What does cisgender mean?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<p>Cisgender, sometimes shortened to cis, is a term for anyone who <strong>consistently identifies</strong> with their sex/gender assigned at birth.</p>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138089-1093df4b-1dd3" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138089-1093df4b-1dd3" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What does two-spirit mean?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<p><span class="TextRun SCXW223088118 BCX0" lang="EN-US" xml:lang="EN-US" data-contrast="auto"><span class="NormalTextRun SCXW223088118 BCX0">Two-spirit is a modern umbrella term used by Native American Indigenous people who are considered neither men nor women, and generally occupy a distinct gender status. </span><span class="NormalTextRun SCXW223088118 BCX0">In many tribes, two-spirit people historically filled special religious roles as healers, shamans and ceremonial leaders. </span><span class="NormalTextRun SCXW223088118 BCX0">Most Indigenous communities have specific terms in their own languages for the gender-diverse people in their communities, and with over 500 surviving Native American cultures, attitudes about sex and gender vary widely. </span></span><span class="EOP SCXW223088118 BCX0" data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></p>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138109-01384327-e99a" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138109-01384327-e99a" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What does nonbinary mean?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Nonbinary is an umbrella term referring to anyone who does not identify within the man/woman or male/female binary. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Some nonbinary people identify as transgender, some do not. These two terms are not mutually exclusive, but rather have large areas of overlap in gender diverse communities.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138130-18fb1f27-39be" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138130-18fb1f27-39be" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What is gender dysphoria?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Gender dysphoria describes </span><strong>the distress that someone may feel when their body and/or social experience does not reflect their gender</strong><b><span data-contrast="auto">. </span></b><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="" data-font="Wingdings" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="3"><span data-contrast="auto">Gender dysphoria is also a DSM V diagnosis (Diagnostic and Statistical Manual, 5</span><span data-contrast="auto">th</span><span data-contrast="auto"> Edition), defined as “a marked incongruence between one’s experienced/expressed gender and assigned gender” which requires </span><i><span data-contrast="auto">clinically significant distress</span></i><span data-contrast="auto"> to be diagnosed.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Dysphoria is treatable</strong><span data-contrast="auto">, largely with appropriate social and/or medical transition to assist with alignment, as well as coping strategies to alleviate distress when dysphoria arises.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Not every trans person experiences gender dysphoria; gender dysphoria is not a requirement to be trans. Dysphoria may wax and wane over moments, days or years.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Trans people can also experience</span> <strong>gender euphoria</strong><span data-contrast="auto">, or the joy and sense of rightness that someone might feel when they are aligned with their body and/or social experience. Colloquially thought of as the opposite, non-pathologizing experience of dysphoria, gender euphoria is used by many transgender & non-binary people to guide their gender journey. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138153-0be0e304-6e51" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138153-0be0e304-6e51" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What does gender have to do with eating disorder treatment?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Transgender people are </span><strong>much more likely to develop eating disorders</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> than cisgender people, with studies estimating that 16% of trans young people have been diagnosed with an eating disorder nationally, roughly 8x more often than their cisgender peers (</span><a href="https://www.jahonline.org/article/S1054-139X(15)00087-7/fulltext"><span data-contrast="none">Diemer et al., 2015</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">). Evidence suggests that this disparity may be due to:</span>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Minority stress (the stress that someone may experience due to being part of a minority, marginalized, or oppressed group);</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Potentially complicated relationships trans people may have to their bodies, such as discomfort or distress within their bodies;</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">A desire to suppress secondary sex characteristics, such as breasts or muscle growth, via malnourishment;</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">And/or a need to find soothing in unsupportive, confusing or closeted situations. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Studies that examine the treatment of eating disorders in trans individuals show that access to </span><strong>gender-affirming care improves outcomes</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/eat.22868"><span data-contrast="none">Donaldson et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.3109/09540261.2015.1089217"><span data-contrast="none">Jones et al., 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/erv.2576"><span data-contrast="none">Jones et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/eat.23539"><span data-contrast="none">Nowaskie et al. 2021</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/hea0000497"><span data-contrast="none">Testa et al., 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">). </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">We want to ensure we are treating the whole person. Eating disorders do not exist in a vacuum, nor can they be separated from the complexity of a human being. For many of our transgender and nonbinary patients, gender identity is an important piece of that complexity and exploring gender becomes a major theme of eating disorder recovery.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559685":1440,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259,"335559991":360}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138176-f4371e7e-25c3" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138176-f4371e7e-25c3" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What is gender-affirmative care at Center for Discovery?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Gender-affirmative care, as practiced by Center for Discovery, refers to care where providers </span><strong>listen to</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> and </span><strong>believe</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> what our patients are telling us about who they are. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Key principles we hold:</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Gender diversity is a normal and wonderful</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> part of human diversity. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Affirmation and support </span><span data-contrast="auto">of someone’s gender identity is associated with</span><span data-contrast="auto"> the best mental health outcomes and has been repeatedly linked to meaningful improvements in eating disorder symptoms, suicide ideation and self-harm</span><span data-contrast="auto"> (</span><a href="https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1111/fare.12304"><span data-contrast="none">Catalpa & McGuire, 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/lgbt.2015.0111"><span data-contrast="none">Klein & Golub, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2016.07.026"><span data-contrast="none">McConnell et al. 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-clinpsy-021815-093153"><span data-contrast="none">Russell & Fish, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-6171.2010.00246.x"><span data-contrast="none">Ryan et al., 2010</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/284551812_Impacts_of_Strong_Parental_Support_for_Trans_Youth"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto"> , </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2016.10.016"><span data-contrast="none">Durwood, McLaughlin, and Olson, 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/fare.12226"><span data-contrast="none">Kuvalanka et al., 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2015-3223"><span data-contrast="none">Olson et al., 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2013.07.019"><span data-contrast="none">Simons et al., 2013</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017, </span></a><a href="https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph17249525"><span data-contrast="none">Sevelius et al., 2020,</span></a> <a href="https://doi.org/10.3109/09540261.2015.1089217"><span data-contrast="none">Jones et al, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/eat.23539"><span data-contrast="none">Nowaskie et al., 2021</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsxm.2018.01.017"><span data-contrast="none">Owen-Smith et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/hea0000497"><span data-contrast="none">Testa et al., 2017, </span></a><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00918369.2012.653300"><span data-contrast="none">de Vries et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/erv.2576"><span data-contrast="none">Jones et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">)</span><span data-contrast="auto">.</span><span data-contrast="auto"> </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Gender identity may shift over time, and we support and affirm that possibility as part of natural human growth and development. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">We support identity exploration throughout the recovery process in every facet of identity, with </span><strong>no attachment to the outcome</strong><span data-contrast="auto">, but rather a belief that </span><strong>affirmation, support and trust in a patient’s self-knowledge</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> will lead them to the life they want to live. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">For all of our patients — cis, trans and questioning — this can include: </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span>
<ul>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Imagining their life is worth living</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Noticing their own preferences</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Identifying and expressing their emotions</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Trying on different identities, appearances, preferences, activities, etc.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Exploring their values</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li><span data-contrast="auto">Developing self-awareness and self-trust. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138202-52767ad4-2e89" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138202-52767ad4-2e89" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">I want my loved one to recover from their eating disorder; why are you focusing so much on their gender?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">We share your desire to help your loved one recover from their eating disorder. In treatment, </span><strong>we focus on whatever we need to focus on to facilitate recovery</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. For some people, this is a strict focus on their eating disorder. For most people, there’s something underlying the eating disorder that keeps them stuck in the eating disorder, so </span><strong>we need to address that underlying issue to support full recovery</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Eating disorders are often coping mechanisms or ways to hide parts of ourselves. If someone is </span><strong>using their eating disorder to cope with dysphoria or using their eating disorder to hide parts of themselves that feels unwelcome</strong><span data-contrast="auto">, confusing or dangerous, then we need to support them in exploring their identity, creating safety, becoming comfortable in uncertainty and coping with dysphoria in other ways.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138230-696a0267-ca87" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138230-696a0267-ca87" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">My loved one has come out has transgender/nonbinary or is questioning their gender; I feel scared/lost/confused/angry/guilty/ashamed, and I don’t know what to do. Help?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><strong>That makes sense!</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> Your loved one exploring or expressing a different gender than what you thought they were is a big deal. You may feel lost, confused, scared, guilty, angry, ashamed, hurt or a wide variety of other emotions, perhaps at the same time as feeling proud, relieved or clarity. This may be outside of your past experiences, maybe outside of the experiences of everyone you know. You may be concerned about what this means for your loved one’s life — you’ve probably heard a lot of scary reports and statistics about how hard it is to be transgender in many communities. This is all so valid and understandable.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="4" data-aria-level="2"><strong>They want you to know them</strong><b><span data-contrast="auto">.</span></b><span data-contrast="auto"> It’s a big deal that they chose to share this with you. They have chosen to let you into this piece of them, saying that they want you to know them better.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="5" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Lead with love.</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> You love your loved one, that is abundantly clear just by the fact that you’re reading this right now. We encourage you to notice all your other emotions and seek support, while keeping your love for them central in all your communication and decision making. What your loved one needs</span><span data-contrast="auto"> to hear and see from you most is your love for them. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="5" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Process your feelings.</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> We want you to have space to process these emotions, perhaps with the support of other family/community members going through the same thing, religious or spiritual community, and/or mental health professionals. We recommend contacting your local PFLAG chapter or other organizations with support groups and experience supporting family members like you, such as Trans Families and Gender Spectrum (see the resources question below). </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813138258-d02ba1e8-2e0c" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813138258-d02ba1e8-2e0c" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">Why is it important to support my loved one?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">As family members, you are uniquely suited to have a </span><strong>huge impact</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> on your loved one’s mental health. Your support can </span><strong>help your child feel confident</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> in themselves and learn to navigate the difficulties of the world from a place of </span><strong>love and resilience</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">If you show support for your loved one’s gender identity, study after study shows that your loved one:</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is less likely to have depression (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2016.10.016"><span data-contrast="none">Durwood et al., 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2015-3223"><span data-contrast="none">Olson et al., 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31318262/"><span data-contrast="none">Puckett et al., 2019</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-clinpsy-021815-093153"><span data-contrast="none">Russell & Fish, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-6171.2010.00246.x"><span data-contrast="none">Ryan et al., 2010</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2013.07.019"><span data-contrast="none">Simons et al., 2013</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/284551812_Impacts_of_Strong_Parental_Support_for_Trans_Youth"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al., 2012;</span></a> <a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017)</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335551550":1,"335551620":1,"335559685":2160,"335559737":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259,"335559991":360}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is less likely to have anxiety (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2016.10.016"><span data-contrast="none">Durwood et al., 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2015-3223"><span data-contrast="none">Olson et al., 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31318262/"><span data-contrast="none">Puckett et al., 2019</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-clinpsy-021815-093153"><span data-contrast="none">Russell & Fish, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017)</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is less likely to misuse substances  (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/lgbt.2015.0111"><span data-contrast="none">Klein & Golub, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; ; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-6171.2010.00246.x"><span data-contrast="none">Ryan et al., 2010)</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is less likely to self-harm (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017)</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is less likely to contemplate or attempt suicide (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-6171.2010.00246.x"><span data-contrast="none">Ryan et al., 2010</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/284551812_Impacts_of_Strong_Parental_Support_for_Trans_Youth"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al., 2012; </span></a><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017)</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is more likely to experience high quality of life (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2013.07.019"><span data-contrast="none">Simons et al., 2013</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2016.07.026"><span data-contrast="none">McConnell et al. 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/284551812_Impacts_of_Strong_Parental_Support_for_Trans_Youth"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">)</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Is more likely to experience high self-esteem (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2016.10.016"><span data-contrast="none">Durwood et al., 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-6171.2010.00246.x"><span data-contrast="none">Ryan et al., 2010;</span></a> <a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/284551812_Impacts_of_Strong_Parental_Support_for_Trans_Youth"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">)</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">And is four times more likely to report good mental health (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017)</span></a><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">In fact, family support is the</span> <strong>strongest predictor of mental health outcomes</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> (</span><a href="https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/31318262/"><span data-contrast="none">Puckett et al., 2019</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">). One study found that only 4% of young trans people with strongly supportive parents attempted suicide in contrast to 57% of trans youth with not strongly supportive parents (</span><a href="https://transpulseproject.ca/wp-content/uploads/2012/10/Impacts-of-Strong-Parental-Support-for-Trans-Youth-vFINAL.pdf"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al, 2012)</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">. Your support of your loved one is critical! We’re here to support you in supporting them.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813785721-6745da5e-4da5" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813785721-6745da5e-4da5" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">How do I get better at using my loved one’s pronouns and name?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">This is a wonderful area to focus your support on immediately. </span><strong>Using your loved one’s name and pronouns is hugely important</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> and we have evidence that being supportive in this way is a protective factor for better mental health outcomes (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2016.10.016"><span data-contrast="none"><span data-ccp-charstyle="Hyperlink">Durwood, McLaughlin, and Olson, 2017</span></span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph17249525"><span data-contrast="none"><span data-ccp-charstyle="Hyperlink">Sevelius</span><span data-ccp-charstyle="Hyperlink"> et al., 2020</span></span></a><span data-contrast="auto">).</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Practice!</strong> <span data-contrast="auto">When you’re driving alone in the car or doing dishes, talk out loud about your loved one, using their name and pronouns. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="4" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Correct yourself</strong><span data-contrast="auto"><strong>.</strong> If you make a mistake, correct yourself, briefly apologize and move on.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="5" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">If your loved one is out to others, then </span><strong>correct others</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> to help them improve their use of your loved one’s pronouns and name. Corrections can be as simple as briefly interrupting to state the correct name or pronoun when you hear errors. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="6" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Making progress on names and pronouns can be something you </span><strong>work on in family therapy</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. Your child’s therapist can help correct you during session, and family therapy is a space to explore how your child experiences misgendering (being referred to as the wrong gender, the wrong name or wrong pronoun).</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813807028-ab88b227-eb01" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813807028-ab88b227-eb01" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What if this is just a phase my loved one is going through?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">We all go through phases — that’s part of life. In adolescence and young adulthood, we explore our identities, and as part of that exploration process, we may “try on” different ways of being. For most people, </span><strong>being trans is not a phase.</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">The evidence suggests that the vast majority of adolescents and adults who do not feel at home in their gender assigned at birth will continue to identify differently than their gender assigned at birth (</span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00918369.2012.653300"><span data-contrast="none">de Vries et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">), though they may move around under the trans umbrella as they continue to explore and come to a deeper understanding of their gender. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Whether or not your loved one identifies in a different way later, your support of their identity exploration and who they are will </span><strong>help them feel safe and loved</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1645813829836-66f4c885-9a4e" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1645813829836-66f4c885-9a4e" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">Isn’t my child too young to know they’re transgender?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul style="font-weight: 400;">
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Sexual and gender exploration is </span><strong>normal at all phases of life</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. We all continue to grow and explore as a lifelong process. There is no right or wrong age to question gender, to come out, or to begin taking steps to align gender identity with body/social experience. Gender exploration is very individual.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Gender identification, or the ability to identify they have a gender and participate in cultural gender norms, begins for most children around ages 2-3. Some children voice experiences of gender incongruence at this young age. Some transgender people realize there is something different about their experience of gender as compared to their peers later in childhood as they navigate gender separated groups.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="4" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">For many transgender adolescents the</span> <strong>onset of puberty</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> often creates significant distress as their body begins to display secondary sex characteristics, leading them to start questioning their gender, identifying as trans, or talking about their gender experiences around puberty.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="5" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Our job is to help your loved one find a sense of peace, clarity, and happiness. In mental health care, we are often responding to a person’s distress, and helping them move from a place of distress to a place of peace, affirmation, clarity, and joy. This is a miraculous and transformative process, </span><strong>regardless of the age it happens</strong><span data-contrast="auto">.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1646177245907-c323861f-62bb" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1646177245907-c323861f-62bb" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">Why didn't my loved one show more signs of being transgender as a child?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul style="font-weight: 400;">
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="6" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Our society has taught us that gender identity is innate and static, never changing but </span><strong>most peoples’ experience of gender is complex and evolving</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. Many of the stories in the media about transgender people fit into that narrative, saying that trans people are “born in the wrong body” and “always knew” they were a different gender than they were assigned. Some people fit this narrative. Many do not.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="7" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Some family members feel surprised when their child comes out as transgender, nonbinary, or questioning of their gender, and family members may look back to their loved one’s childhood to try to process what their child is telling them. For some families, they can find memories that help them feel that their child coming out makes sense. For others, they find it difficult to reconcile how they experienced their loved one in childhood with what their loved one is telling them now. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="8" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">If your loved one is telling you that their gender questioning or gender identity is something they have had for a long time, </span><strong>it might be that your loved one was skilled at hiding their questioning from everyone and this helped them protect themselves</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. Children pick up early on what the expectations are for their assigned gender, and they learn early that if they do not follow those expectations, bad things may happen. They may have learned that they needed to act as their assigned gender in order to be loved, to be safe, and to survive. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="9" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">It is also </span><strong>common for people’s relationship with gender to change over time.</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> Many children don’t have any discomfort in their body and assigned gender before puberty, and the changes of puberty are when discomfort starts. For some people, their assigned gender fits well enough to not have excessive distress, but later they found other gender options that fit better. For others, their gender changed as they grew and changed, much the way that their personality, values, interests or other aspects of their identity might change.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="10" data-aria-level="2"><strong>It is normal to look for answers and certainty, but that isn’t always possible right away</strong><span data-contrast="auto"><strong>.</strong> It is normal to feel confused and distressed if you don’t find answers and certainty. We encourage you to find support as you work through any feelings </span><strong>that arise for you as your loved one explores their identity.</strong></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1646177422561-93fc8e14-dedf" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1646177422561-93fc8e14-dedf" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">Isn’t it best to wait until my child is 18 or older to make any permanent decisions about their gender or to transition?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul style="font-weight: 400;">
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="11" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">If you are reading this question, it is likely that your child has let you know that they need to start one or more transition steps (steps to align their body and/or social experience to their gender). It is understandable that you may want to wait for your child to be old enough to legally make their own decisions, for you to be more comfortable with the steps they want to take, or to see if they change their mind. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="12" data-aria-level="2"><strong>When someone is in significant psychological distress, we need to take steps to alleviate that distress.</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> When a person lets us know that they need to take transition steps, it usually means that they are experiencing significant distress with their current situation. You may have noticed this distress, through their words, emotions and/or behavior, including eating disorder behaviors, substance use behaviors, self-harm behaviors or suicidal behaviors. We want to alleviate that distress. Talk with your loved one and their treatment team to get individualized recommendations about how ongoing gender affirmation through social transition, medical transition and/or legal document updates could improve their mental health struggles.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="13" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Everyone has a different path and each person needs different support to alleviate their distress.</strong> <span data-contrast="auto">Many people will need to experiment, start and stop interventions, or continue to course-correct as they assess what the right decisions are for them. It’s valuable to focus on </span><strong>increasing joy and decreasing distress</strong><span data-contrast="auto">, at a pace set by your loved one, rather than adherence to any particular path or destination.</span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1646177462071-bd1dd1ad-8900" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1646177462071-bd1dd1ad-8900" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What if my loved one has regrets?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Many family members have concerns that their loved one may later regret transition steps that they take. It is normal and understandable to have concerns about your loved one’s well-being and how choices they make now will affect their life going forward. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Rates of regret are very low</strong><span data-contrast="auto">, with current estimates ranging </span><a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/262734734_An_Analysis_of_All_Applications_for_Sex_Reassignment_Surgery_in_Sweden_1960-2010_Prevalence_Incidence_and_Regrets" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">between</span></a> <a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsxm.2018.01.016" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">less than</span></a> <a href="https://epath.eu/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/Boof-of-abstracts-EPATH2019.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">1%</span></a> <a href="https://doi.org/10.1097/gox.0000000000003477" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">and</span></a> <a href="https://transequality.org/sites/default/files/docs/usts/USTS-Full-Report-Dec17.pdf" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">3.8%</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">. That means that there is a 96.2-99.5% chance that your child will not regret these transition steps.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><strong>Most transition steps are completely or partially reversible</strong><span data-contrast="auto"><strong>.</strong> If your loved one needs to make a different decision later, they can do so.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="4" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">As Myles Marcotte, ASW, discusses in his training, </span><a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_yo5ci_I5SM" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Normalizing Complex Grief in Transition</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">, it is often assumed that if someone transitions and then later wants to transition back/again, they will feel regret about having transitioned in the first place, and that this regret will cause suffering. However, </span><strong>neither regret nor suffering are inevitable</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. We all make decisions throughout our lives, and when we move onto a different path, we can view the paths we have been on as growing and learning experiences. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li style="list-style-type: none;">
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="" data-font="Wingdings" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="3"><span data-contrast="auto">If we do regret past choices, we can process that with therapists, family and friends, and work towards acceptance and forgiveness of our past and present. This is the work all people, regardless of gender identity, do throughout our lives, for all sorts of decisions. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1646177498526-13e50d04-884c" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1646177498526-13e50d04-884c" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">What about Rapid Onset Gender Dysphoria? I’ve heard that affirming my child will confuse and harm them.</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">Rapid Onset Gender Dysphoria (ROGD) was a concept proposed by one researcher in a survey of parents in 2017; </span><strong>this concept has been widely challenged by experts in the field of transgender care</strong><span data-contrast="auto">. In the survey, parents reported being surprised by their children coming out, and talked about their caregiver perspective that their child’s gender dysphoria or transgender identification appeared suddenly. As other parents and some providers recognized their experiences in this paper, this theory of ROGD circulated more and more widely.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>
<ul>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="1" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">While we recognize the importance of understanding and supporting parents’ experiences, we also know that parents’ perceptions of their children’s experiences may not be completely accurate. When a teen or young adult comes out to their family, they may have been thinking about their gender for a while, though it may be a complete surprise to their parents. There are many reasons that a child may not share their gender questioning with their parents: they may be afraid of rejection, they may have learned that questioning their gender is wrong (from family, faith spaces or the world at large), or they may not have had words to put to feelings they’ve been experiencing. </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="2" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">The theory of ROGD has been being researched since it was proposed, and a recent study by </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpeds.2021.11.020" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="auto">Bauer et al published in November of 2021</span></a><span data-contrast="auto"> found</span> <strong>no evidence supporting the existence of ROGD as a distinct form of gender dysphoria</strong><span data-contrast="auto">.</span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
<li data-leveltext="o" data-font="Courier New" data-listid="2" aria-setsize="-1" data-aria-posinset="3" data-aria-level="2"><span data-contrast="auto">At Center for Discovery, we are always monitoring the most up-to-date research to ensure that we are providing the best possible care. </span><strong>Currently, the best evidence we have is that professional and familial support and affirmation results in best outcomes</strong><span data-contrast="auto"> (</span><a href="https://psycnet.apa.org/doi/10.1111/fare.12304" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Catalpa & McGuire, 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/lgbt.2015.0111" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Klein & Golub, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2016.07.026" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">McConnell et al. 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-clinpsy-021815-093153" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Russell & Fish, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1744-6171.2010.00246.x" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Ryan et al., 2010</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://www.researchgate.net/publication/284551812_Impacts_of_Strong_Parental_Support_for_Trans_Youth" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Travers et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto"> , </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jaac.2016.10.016" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Durwood, McLaughlin, and Olson, 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00224499.2017.1415291" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Katz-Wise et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/fare.12226" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Kuvalanka et al., 2017</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2015-3223" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Olson et al., 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jadohealth.2013.07.019" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Simons et al., 2013</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1089/trgh.2017.0031" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Veale et al., 2017, </span></a><a href="https://doi.org/10.3390/ijerph17249525" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Sevelius et al., 2020,</span></a> <a href="https://doi.org/10.3109/09540261.2015.1089217" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Jones et al, 2016</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/eat.23539" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Nowaskie et al., 2021</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jsxm.2018.01.017" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Owen-Smith et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.apa.org/doi/10.1037/hea0000497" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Testa et al., 2017, </span></a><a href="https://doi.org/10.1080/00918369.2012.653300" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">de Vries et al., 2012</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">; </span><a href="https://doi.org/10.1002/erv.2576" target="_blank" rel="noopener"><span data-contrast="none">Jones et al., 2018</span></a><span data-contrast="auto">). </span><span data-ccp-props="{"201341983":0,"335559739":160,"335559740":259}"> </span></li>
</ul>

		</div>
	</div>
</div></div><div class="vc_tta-panel" id="1646177560339-7ccca8b8-cc11" data-vc-content=".vc_tta-panel-body"><div class="vc_tta-panel-heading"><h4 class="vc_tta-panel-title vc_tta-controls-icon-position-left"><a href="#1646177560339-7ccca8b8-cc11" data-vc-accordion data-vc-container=".vc_tta-container"><span class="vc_tta-title-text">My child only started identifying this way after socializing with other transgender and nonbinary kids at school- isn't this really about peer pressure and popularity?</span><i class="vc_tta-controls-icon vc_tta-controls-icon-plus"></i></a></h4></div><div class="vc_tta-panel-body">
	<div class="wpb_text_column wpb_content_element " >
		<div class="wpb_wrapper">
			<ul style="font-weight: 400;">
